metadata.title: Research Cancer Immunoediting with Claude: Elimination, Equilibrium, and Escape

beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Salut, Liam — in for Bear. The cancer you see in the clinic is the one that won? Can you explain it, Bear?

props.command: The cancer you see in the clinic is the one that won?
props.segment: Research Cancer Immunoediting with Claude: Elimination,

beats.narration_text: In 2001, Robert Schreiber's lab showed that immunodeficient mice developed more cancers more quickly than immunocompetent mice — proving the immune system is not a passenger in cancer development. It actively suppresses tumor growth. The cancer you see in the clinic is the one that won.

The three-phase immunoediting framework: Elimination — immune surveillance recognizes and destroys pre-cancerous cells via CD8+ T cells, NK cells, and interferon-γ-driven apoptosis. Equilibrium — the longest and most clinically invisible phase: residual cancer cells are held in dormancy under immune pressure, neither eliminated nor able to expand. Escape — cancer cells acquire immune evasion mechanisms: MHC class I downregulation, PD-L1 upregulation, antigen loss. The tumor in the clinic emerged from escape.
Ask Claude to map the three immunoediting phases.

props.command: claude "Research the three phases of cancer immunoediting (elimination, equilibrium, escape). For each: explain the molecular mechanism, what experimental evidence supports it, and what clinical observation corresponds. Then explain how understanding escape mechanisms led directly to the design of checkpoint inhibitors."

props.code:
# immunoediting_brief.md  --  Claude output

## ELIMINATION
- Shankaran et al. 2001 (Science)
- RAG2-/- mice: more and faster MCA sarcomas
- Mechanism: IFN-γ-dependent recognition, CD8+/NK cell killing

## EQUILIBRIUM
- LCMV dormancy model — TIL pressure without elimination
- Clinical correlate: occult micrometastases post-resection
- Longest and most clinically invisible phase

## ESCAPE
- MHC I downregulation: present in 60-80% of cancers
- PD-L1 upregulation: driven by IFN-γ — immune induces suppression
- Antigen loss: selective pressure eliminates recognized neoantigens
- Design rationale for checkpoint inhibitors: block PD-1/PD-L1 brake

beats.narration_text: The three-phase timeline animates: elimination phase in gold — CD8+ T cells, NK cells, IFN-γ. Equilibrium phase in grey — dormancy, immune balance, the longest phase. Escape phase in crimson — MHC-I downregulation, PD-L1 upregulation, antigen loss. Clinical cancer arrow drops at the end of escape. Citation: Schreiber lab 2001 in Science.
Ask Claude about the equilibrium phase as a prevention target.

props.command: claude "Identify the equilibrium phase as therapeutically interesting — could drugs that maintain immune equilibrium prevent cancer escape? What evidence exists that adjuvant immunotherapy operates by reinforcing equilibrium rather than achieving elimination?"

beats.narration_text: Equilibrium-maintenance concept: Equilibrium represents a stable state where immune pressure prevents cancer expansion without eliminating cancer cells. Therapeutic maintenance of equilibrium — rather than cure — is an emerging cancer prevention concept. Evidence: adjuvant checkpoint inhibitors in resected melanoma and NSCLC maintain higher disease-free survival, possibly by restoring elimination from near-equilibrium micrometastases. Aspirin's colorectal cancer prevention may partly operate by maintaining anti-tumor immune surveillance — COX-2 suppression reduces prostaglandin-mediated T cell suppression in pre-malignant tissue. No drug is approved specifically to maintain equilibrium — but the framework reframes adjuvant immunotherapy as equilibrium reinforcement.

The immunoediting framework explains why the cancer you see in the clinic is always the one that already evaded detection. The tumor is not a random sample of all possible cancers — it is the survivor of selection by the immune system. MHC class I downregulation is found in 60-80% of cancers because every cancer that didn't acquire it was eliminated. Understanding this changes how you think about early detection and prevention: you're looking for tumors before they complete the escape phase.
Next: research Australia's cervical cancer elimination model — proof that a vaccine can eliminate a cancer before it starts.

beats.title: Your turn handoff — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Take this prompt, run it on your own — pick any cancer type or clinical scenario you know about and ask how this mechanism applies there.

props.command: Explain how [Research Cancer Immunoediting with Claude: Elimination, Equilibrium, a] applies to a specific cancer type or clinical case you're studying. What proteins are involved, what goes wrong at the molecular level, and what is the therapeutic or diagnostic significance?
